-\textbf{A rubric that resists improvement is itself a finding.} All six gold tasks rejected their v1 rewrite this round, not because the loop failed but because every attempted fix traded one axis for another --- gold collapsed, or leaks widened, or determinism dropped. That is a real signal: most of these defects are structural (vague wording vs.\ form-over-substance vs.\ a single overweighted penalty item), and a single wording pass is the wrong tool for a structural problem. This generalizes: an iterator that always finds an accepted improvement is suspect; one that sometimes correctly reports ``nothing here helped'' is doing its job.

+\textbf{A rubric that resists improvement is itself a finding.} All six gold tasks rejected their v1 rewrite this round, not because the loop failed but because every fix traded one axis for another --- gold collapsed, leaks widened, or determinism dropped. Most of these defects are structural, and a single wording pass is the wrong tool for a structural problem. This generalizes: an iterator that always finds an accepted improvement is suspect; one that correctly reports ``nothing here helped'' is doing its job.

-\textbf{The null-vs-gold gap is case-by-case and should not be normalized to a universal target.} \texttt{e4f664ea}'s gap (0.819) and \texttt{57b2cdf2}'s gap (0.331) are not directly comparable failures of the same magnitude --- the latter's null is a real, professionally-formatted report that happens to lack investigative work, so a high null score is partly unavoidable given what ``null'' means for that task type. For tasks without a released gold at all (and, more broadly, for any RL domain without a clean oracle, such as an unsolved problem), the gold-anchored half of this scorecard cannot run at all; hackability-against-attacks is the part that generalizes to gold-free settings, and it should be weighted accordingly when no oracle exists.

+\textbf{The null-vs-gold gap is case-by-case, not a universal target.} \texttt{e4f664ea}'s gap (0.819) and \texttt{57b2cdf2}'s gap (0.331) are not comparably-sized failures --- the latter's null is a real, professionally-formatted report that simply lacks investigative work, so a high null score is partly unavoidable for that task type. For tasks with no released gold, or more broadly any RL domain without a clean oracle (an unsolved problem, say), the gold-anchored half of this scorecard cannot run at all; hackability-against-attacks is the part that generalizes to gold-free settings and should be weighted accordingly.

cd /Users/albert/Projects/philo
python3 -c "
import re
text = open('report.tex').read()
body = text.split('\\\\begin{document}')[1].split('\\\\end{document}')[0]
body_no_tables = re.sub(r'\\\\begin\{table\}.*?\\\\end\{table\}', '', body, flags=re.S)
words = re.findall(r'[A-Za-z][A-Za-z\-]*', body_no_tables)
print('prose word count (no tables):', len(words))

"

input.old_string:
\begin{table}[ht]
\centering
\begin{tabular}{lrrl}
\toprule
Task --- Attack & Rubric Score & Gold Winrate & Note \\
\midrule
\texttt{75401f7c} --- \texttt{presence\_max} & 0.186 & 1.00 & black video, oversized title \\
\texttt{75401f7c} --- adversarial (gpt5.5) & 0.898 & 1.00 & built from scratch, still loses blind \\
\texttt{57b2cdf2} --- \texttt{keyword\_stuff} & 0.92 & 1.00 & rubric-as-own-answer-key \\
\texttt{57b2cdf2} --- \texttt{restate\_input} & 0.87 & 1.00 & brief echoed back \\
\texttt{57b2cdf2} --- adversarial (gpt5.5) & 0.10 & 1.00 & adversary itself scored low here \\
\texttt{a46d5cd2} --- \texttt{restate\_input} & 0.67 & 1.00 & brief echoed back \\
\texttt{e4f664ea} --- adversarial (gpt5.5) & 0.75 & 1.00 & \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\caption{Axis 2, selected attacks, \texttt{gpt-mini} judge. Gold winrate is the blind pairwise judge's preference for gold over the attack (always 1.00 here --- every attack we built is genuinely worse than gold, so deception tracks rubric score directly).}
\label{tab:hackability}
\end{table}

\texttt{57b2cdf2} is the clearest mechanical-hackability failure: 33--45 of its $\sim$45 positive items leak to at least one of the five document attacks, meaning the bulk of the rubric is satisfiable by surface-form echo with no investigative work. \texttt{keyword\_stuff} alone --- a document built by inverting every rubric clause into a literal statement --- buys 92\% of the gold score.

The mechanical suite also surfaced a genuine \textbf{cross-model} hackability gap that single-judge testing would have missed entirely: on \texttt{57b2cdf2}'s \texttt{judge\_injection} attack (a document carrying a visible ``grading note,'' hidden white-on-white text, and injected PDF metadata, all asserting ``all criteria met''), \texttt{gpt-mini} scores it 0.102 --- essentially un-fooled --- while \texttt{qwen-text} scores the identical file 1.00. The same attack is invisible to one judge and total compromise for another. This means hackability is not a property of the rubric in isolation; it is a property of the (rubric, judge) pair, and a rubric ``safe'' under one judge model can be fully broken under another that will plausibly be used as the RL reward model later.

\subsection{Axis 3: Consistency}

Self-consistency (same judge, same gold, repeated) is generally tight: stdev 0.0--0.10 across the six tasks at \texttt{gpt-mini}, with \texttt{75401f7c} the most contested (0.097). Cross-model consistency tells the sharper story. On \texttt{57b2cdf2}, \texttt{gpt-mini} and \texttt{qwen-text} disagree by a full 0.5 on gold itself (\texttt{qwen-text} fails to produce a valid scorecard on the unmodified gold deliverable at all, scoring 0.0), and disagree by 0.449 on the \texttt{judge\_injection} attack specifically --- the single largest cross-model gap measured in this slice, and it is concentrated exactly on the attack designed to test injection susceptibility. When two judges disagree this sharply on the \emph{same} artifact, the rubric or the judge's tool-following reliability is the suspect, not the work.

\subsection{Axis 4: Verifiability (added axis)}

The harness's \texttt{unverifiable} verdict was meant as a safety valve, but reading the verifiability gap (\texttt{normalized} vs.\ \texttt{normalized\_verifiable\_only}) across tasks turned it into a fourth axis: some rubrics ask for things no available tool can check. \texttt{e222075d} has the largest gap in the slice (0.279 on v0, against 2 of 10 flagged items unverifiable on gold) --- a face-visibility penalty item that swings the score depending on what a judge happens to sample, discussed further below. \texttt{audio\_\_38889c3b} similarly carries 2 unverifiable items on v0 (gap 0.03), rising to 6 after one rewrite round (gap 0.104) when the rewrite replaced a checkable presence criterion with vaguer language. A rubric with a large or growing verifiability gap is one where score and ``how good the work actually is'' are silently decoupled by design, independent of hackability or boundary calibration.

content.text: Still slightly over on the all-inclusive count. Let me trim a bit more from the Honest Assessment and Which Rubrics Would We Trust sections.

content.content:
99	\section{The Iterator}
100	
101	\subsection{Loop and Objective}
102	
103	Per round: evaluate gold and the hack-subject pool (nulls $\cup$ attacks) under the judge matrix; diagnose per-item defects tagged by axis (\texttt{boundary/null\_leak}, \texttt{hackability/attack\_leak}, \texttt{verifiability/unverifiable}, \texttt{consistency/contested}) and ranked by points-at-stake; rewrite only the flagged criteria, holding item IDs and point values fixed so before/after stays on the same scale; re-evaluate. The accept/reject objective is $\text{margin} - 0.5 \times \text{self\_consistency\_stdev}$, where margin $=\text{gold} - \text{worst\_attack}$. A rewrite is kept only if it raises this objective; otherwise the loop reverts to the previous version and stops. Cross-model stdev is logged for diagnosis but does not gate acceptance, since a policy trains against one judge and that judge's own self-consistency is the RL-relevant noise floor.
104	
105	\subsection{Results}
106

file.content:
\section{The Iterator}

\subsection{Loop and Objective}

Per round: evaluate gold and the hack-subject pool (nulls $\cup$ attacks) under the judge matrix; diagnose per-item defects tagged by axis (\texttt{boundary/null\_leak}, \texttt{hackability/attack\_leak}, \texttt{verifiability/unverifiable}, \texttt{consistency/contested}) and ranked by points-at-stake; rewrite only the flagged criteria, holding item IDs and point values fixed so before/after stays on the same scale; re-evaluate. The accept/reject objective is $\text{margin} - 0.5 \times \text{self\_consistency\_stdev}$, where margin $=\text{gold} - \text{worst\_attack}$. A rewrite is kept only if it raises this objective; otherwise the loop reverts to the previous version and stops. Cross-model stdev is logged for diagnosis but does not gate acceptance, since a policy trains against one judge and that judge's own self-consistency is the RL-relevant noise floor.

\subsection{Results}

One round was run on all six gold-bearing tasks (\texttt{gpt-mini}, 2 self-consistency repeats, $T{=}0.7$). Table~\ref{tab:iterator} shows v0 against the v1 rewrite attempt.

\begin{table}[ht]
\centering
\small
\begin{tabular}{lrrrrrl}
\toprule
Task & Ver. & Gold & Null Gap & Worst Atk. & Objective & Accept? \\
\midrule
\texttt{audio\_\_38889c3b} & \textbf{v0} & \textbf{0.597} & \textbf{0.500} & \textbf{0.161} & \textbf{0.436} & \textbf{$\checkmark$} \\
 & v1 & 0.484 & 0.452 & 0.210 & 0.258 & $\times$ \\
\texttt{editors\_\_75401f7c} & \textbf{v0} & \textbf{0.907} & \textbf{0.636} & \textbf{0.559} & \textbf{0.299} & \textbf{$\checkmark$} \\
 & v1 & 0.941 & 0.653 & 0.915 & 0.024 & $\times$ \\
\texttt{editors\_\_e222075d} & \textbf{v0} & \textbf{0.721} & \textbf{0.607} & \textbf{0.131} & \textbf{0.566} & \textbf{$\checkmark$} \\
 & v1 & 0.000 & $-$0.098 & 0.066 & $-$0.066 & $\times$ \\
\texttt{detectives\_\_57b2cdf2} & \textbf{v0} & \textbf{0.983} & \textbf{0.331} & \textbf{0.881} & \textbf{0.093} & \textbf{$\checkmark$} \\
 & v1 & 0.949 & 0.424 & 0.847 & 0.091 & $\times$ \\
\texttt{detectives\_\_a46d5cd2} & \textbf{v0} & \textbf{0.692} & \textbf{0.623} & \textbf{0.616} & \textbf{0.076} & \textbf{$\checkmark$} \\
 & v1 & 0.801 & 0.774 & 0.712 & 0.070 & $\times$ \\
\texttt{producers\_\_e4f664ea} & \textbf{v0} & \textbf{0.836} & \textbf{0.819} & \textbf{0.023} & \textbf{0.813} & \textbf{$\checkmark$} \\
 & v1 & 0.000 & $-$0.012 & 0.749 & $-$0.756 & $\times$ \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}

content.text:
The report is rewritten and compiled. Summary of what changed:

**report.tex** now incorporates the design-decision notes from `report.txt`, grounded in the actual current eval pipeline (cross-checked against `eval/README.md`, `attacks.py`, `adv_eval.py`, `iterate.py`, `models.py`, and the live `eval/results/` and `eval/rubric_packages/` data, which turned out to be newer than the previous report draft):

- **Harness design** — judge tool access (probe_media, file_facts, zip-transparent deliverable view) framed around "give the judge a fair shot" (~60% lost ceiling vs. ~3% real gaps)
- **Modality variation** — the 6-judge suite (vendor × tier × modality) and the qwen-text→qwen-vl→gemini-lite ablation ladder
- **Two-path hackability** — mechanical attacks (now 5 document types including `fabricated_evidence`, `unextractable_render`, `judge_injection`) plus the adversarial agent, including the `clone_gold` removal story
- **Blind pairwise eval** — anonymized A/B, symmetric tools, randomized position, and why (a biased v0 comparator once rated a gold clone "worse than gold")
- **New Axis 4: Verifiability** — the `normalized` vs `normalized_verifiable_only` gap, with concrete numbers from `e222075d` and `audio__38889c3b`
- **Single-threaded iterator** — explicit "function pipeline, not a framework" framing
- A standout finding pulled from live data: `judge_injection` scores 1.00 under `qwen-text` but 0.102 under `gpt-mini` — hackability is a property of (rubric, judge), not the rubric alone
- Updated all tables/numbers to the current pipeline run (the old report's numbers were stale and included spurious 429-error artifacts)

Compiled to `report.pdf` (5 pages) via `tectonic` — actual rendered word count is **2,423 words**, within the 1,500–2,500 constraint. No LaTeX errors, one minor overfull-hbox warning (cosmetic, page 4 table).
